topic: waka ama crew entering a regatta — training intensity

turns.content: so our waka ama crew has entered a regatta and training just went from regular to intense and i'm wondering how to manage the workload without falling apart

That's exciting! A regatta is a real goal to train toward, which can be great for motivation but also ramps up the physical demands quickly. How much has training increased compared to what you were doing before, and when's the regatta?

the regatta is mid-april so we've got about four weeks. we've added an extra session on saturdays which is a longer endurance paddle, and the regular sessions have gotten harder too — more sprint intervals, more focus on race pace. my body was just starting to feel comfortable with the normal load and now it's been cranked up eh

Four weeks is a tight window to peak for a race, but it's doable if the training is structured well. The fact that your body had adapted to the regular load is actually a good foundation — you've built the base fitness and now you're layering race-specific work on top of it.

The Saturday endurance session makes sense for building the cardiovascular capacity you'll need for a regatta distance. The sprint intervals during the week build your top-end speed and teach your body to recover while still paddling.

What's worrying you more — the physical toll, or fitting it all in around your life?

both honestly. physically i'm managing but the fatigue is building. my arms are heavy by friday and then saturday is the long session and i'm starting the next week still tired. and then there's work — i can't exactly tell a mama in labour to hold on because i've got tired shoulders from paddling eh. i need to be sharp when i'm on call

That's a real tension. Your work isn't a desk job where you can coast on a tired day — you need to be physically and mentally present, sometimes for hours, sometimes in the middle of the night. Accumulated fatigue from training is a genuine concern when your job requires that kind of readiness.

A few things to think about:

**Recovery as training:** In a four-week ramp-up, recovery is just as important as the hard sessions. If you're starting each week still carrying fatigue from the last, you're not recovering enough between sessions. Are you getting enough sleep, eating well, stretching and rolling out after sessions?

**Talk to your coach or crew leader:** Let them know about your work demands. A good coach will understand that one crew member might need to modify intensity on certain days — maybe you do the Saturday long paddle at a lower intensity rather than race pace, or you drop one midweek session in the heavier weeks.

**Non-negotiable rest:** Give yourself at least one full rest day where you do nothing physical. Your body needs that window to repair.

Have you talked to anyone in the crew about how you're feeling?

not yet because i don't want to be the one who can't handle the training. everyone else seems to be getting on with it and i feel like if i say i'm struggling they'll think i'm not committed. which is stupid because i know they're not like that but the feeling is still there eh

That feeling is really common, especially when you're newer to a sport and training alongside people who've been doing it longer. But I'd push back on the idea that speaking up means you can't handle it. It actually means you're being smart about managing competing demands — and the crew needs you healthy and strong on race day, not broken from overtraining.

I'd bet if you mentioned it, at least two other people would say they're feeling it too. Nobody breezes through a training ramp-up. And your situation is genuinely different from someone who has a predictable nine-to-five — being on call for births means your recovery can be disrupted at any time through no fault of your own.

Maybe frame it to the crew as a practical thing rather than a complaint: "I need to manage my energy because of my work, can we talk about how I adjust on the heavier weeks?" That's responsible, not weak.

you're right, i know you're right. and actually our steerer is really good about that kind of thing, she's always checking in on how everyone's feeling in the waka and she'd rather we spoke up than pushed through and got injured before the regatta. i'll talk to her after our next session

Good — and the steerer is the right person to go to. She has the overview of the whole crew's condition and she can make adjustments without singling you out. An injury four weeks before a regatta would be far worse for the crew than you dialling back one session a week.

In the meantime, focus on what you can control between sessions: hydration, protein after training, sleep quality, and stretching. Those things compound over four weeks and they'll make the difference between arriving at the regatta feeling sharp or arriving depleted.

protein and sleep i can do, stretching i'm terrible at because i always run out of time in the mornings trying to get sorted for the day. i paddle and then it's straight into getting ready for work or getting Maia sorted for school. there's no gap for a proper cool-down

That's a scheduling problem more than a discipline problem. If your mornings go straight from the water into mum mode and work mode, the stretching needs to happen at a different time.

Try this: ten minutes of stretching in the evening instead. After Maia's in bed, before you wind down for the night. Focus on shoulders, lats, upper back, and hip flexors. It doesn't need to be a big production — just a few held stretches on the floor while you watch something or listen to music. Evening stretching also helps with sleep quality, which feeds back into recovery.

If even that feels like too much, five minutes is better than zero. Your muscles don't care when you stretch them, just that you do.

evening stretching i can actually do, that's a much better fit for my life. i'll try it tonight. one more thing — is there anything specific i should be doing nutrition-wise in the lead-up? i eat pretty well normally but i'm wondering if the extra training means i should be eating differently

With the increased training load, the main thing is making sure you're eating enough overall. It's easy to undereat when you're training hard, especially if your appetite is suppressed by fatigue, and that slows recovery down.

Specifically:
- **Carbs before training:** Your muscles need glycogen for the sprint intervals and the long endurance sessions. A banana or some toast before you head out is enough — nothing heavy.
- **Protein after training:** Within an hour of finishing if you can. This is the repair window. A smoothie, eggs, leftover meat from dinner — whatever's easy.
- **Hydration:** You're sweating even if you're cold, and dehydration makes fatigue worse. Keep a water bottle with you through the day.

You don't need supplements or special powders. Just eat real food, eat enough of it, and time your protein around sessions. Are you eating breakfast after your morning paddles?

honestly some mornings i skip it because i'm rushing to get Maia to school and myself to work. i know that's terrible especially after a hard session but the mornings just disappear on me. i'll make more of an effort though — even if it's just a smoothie in the car on the way to drop her off, something is better than nothing eh

A smoothie in the car is perfect — blend some banana, milk, a handful of oats and some peanut butter the night before and keep it in the fridge. Grab it on the way out. That takes thirty seconds and gives you everything you need after a session. The mornings will always be tight but feeding yourself isn't optional when you're training this hard.

Alright so the plan is — talk to the steerer about managing intensity, evening stretches, sleep and nutrition, smoothie in the car, and stop trying to be superwoman about the whole thing. You've got this.

i've got this. i'm really looking forward to this regatta even though i'm complaining about the training eh. being out on the moana with the crew racing together, i can't think of anything i'd rather be doing. thanks for talking me through all of it

The fact that you're excited even while you're tired tells you everything about whether this is the right thing for you. The training is the price of admission and you're willing to pay it — you just need to be smart about how you pay it so you've still got energy left for the things that matter most. Talk to your steerer, stretch in the evenings, and trust the process. Four weeks from now you'll be on that start line with your crew and none of the sore mornings will matter. Kia kaha out there.
